message:
chore(beads): file numpy.ma/minimum_fill_value and masked_invalid parity follow-ups

Capture three new task beads that surfaced while reviewing numpy.ma
coverage so the ready queue reflects the next parity targets:

- franken_numpy-9ber: numpy.ma python parity for minimum_fill_value
  (the wrapper layer that exposes the existing internal helper to
  fnp-python callers still needs a public binding).
- franken_numpy-lqpc: numpy.ma masked_invalid wrapper parity — match
  NumPy's keep_mask/copy semantics on the public surface.
- franken_numpy-pb53: numpy.linalg lstsq python wrapper parity — the
  solver core exists but the high-level lstsq entrypoint isn't yet
  wired through fnp-python.

No code or behavior changes — pure issue-tracker bookkeeping so agents
can pick these up via br ready.

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"franken_numpy-9ah","title":"Document all security/resource bound constants with rationale comments","description":"## Problem

The project has multiple MAX_* constants for security and resource bounding. While they are already named constants (not bare magic numbers), most lack documentation explaining WHY these specific values were chosen and what attack or resource-exhaustion scenario they defend against.

## Constants Needing Documentation

### fnp-io (crates/fnp-io/src/lib.rs)
- `MAX_HEADER_BYTES = 65_536` — NPY header size cap. Why 64KB? NumPy uses 2^16 as header_len field max in v1.0 format. Document this.
- `MAX_ARCHIVE_MEMBERS = 4_096` — NPZ member count cap. Why 4096? Arbitrary but reasonable. Document the threat (archive bomb with millions of tiny files).
- `MAX_ARCHIVE_UNCOMPRESSED_BYTES = 2 * 1024 * 1024 * 1024` — 2GB uncompressed cap. Document the threat (zip bomb with high compression ratio).
- `MAX_TEXT_ELEMENTS = 16 * 1024 * 1024` — 16M elements for text IO. Document: this limits loadtxt/genfromtxt to ~128MB of f64 data.
- `MAX_DISPATCH_RETRIES = 8` — IO dispatch retries. What is being retried? Document the retry scenario.
- `MAX_MEMMAP_VALIDATION_RETRIES = 64` — Memmap validation retries. Why 64? Document what can fail and why retries help.

### fnp-random (crates/fnp-random/src/lib.rs)
- `MAX_SEED_SEQUENCE_POOL_SIZE = 256` — Why 256? NumPy uses 4 u32 words by default; 256 is far larger. Document the reasoning.
- `MAX_SEED_SEQUENCE_CHILDREN = 4096` — Why 4096? Document the spawn tree depth/breadth this supports.
- `MAX_SEED_SEQUENCE_WORDS = 1_048_576` — 1M words. Document: this is the max state size for generate_state().
- `MAX_RNG_JUMP_OPERATIONS = 1024` — Why 1024? Document what a "jump" costs and why limiting is needed.
- `MAX_RNG_STATE_SCHEMA_FIELDS = 4096` — State serialization limit. Document the threat (malformed state with millions of fields).
- `MAX_BINOMIAL_DIRECT_TRIALS = i64::MAX as u64` — Documents the int-to-float boundary for binomial.

### fnp-linalg (crates/fnp-linalg/src/lib.rs)
- `MAX_TOLERANCE_SEARCH_DEPTH = 128` — What tolerance search? Document the algorithm.
- `MAX_BACKEND_REVALIDATION_ATTEMPTS = 64` — What backend? Document the scenario.
- `MAX_BATCH_SHAPE_CHECKS = 2_000_000` — Why 2M? Document: prevents O(n²) broadcast checks from hanging.

### fnp-ufunc (crates/fnp-ufunc/src/lib.rs)
- `MAX_EXACT_OFFSETS = 200_000` — Used in shares_memory exact-path offset collection. Document why 200K.
- `MAX_EXACT_F64_I64/U64/INTEGER = 2^53` — Already well-understood (IEEE 754 mantissa). Could use a one-liner doc.

## Proposed Fix

For each constant, add a `///` doc comment with:
1. What security threat or resource-exhaustion scenario it defends against
2. How the specific value was chosen (NumPy parity, empirical testing, or arbitrary-but-reasonable)
3. What happens when the limit is exceeded (error type, behavior)

## Validation

No behavioral changes — documentation only. Clippy and tests should be unaffected.

## Files to Modify

- `crates/fnp-io/src/lib.rs`
- `crates/fnp-random/src/lib.rs`
- `crates/fnp-linalg/src/lib.rs`
- `crates/fnp-ufunc/src/lib.rs`","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-25T03:04:52.527989323Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-25T03:34:12.021935165Z","closed_at":"2026-03-25T03:34:12.021338505Z","close_reason":"Added doc comments to all 18 security/resource bound constants across fnp-io (6), fnp-random (7), fnp-linalg (3), fnp-ufunc (1). Each documents the threat defended against, rationale for the specific value, and behavior on limit exceeded.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"franken_numpy-9ah","depends_on_id":"franken_numpy-z8x","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-25T03:06:49.212288182Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

 {"id":"franken_numpy-w4zs","title":"Cross-engine optimization wave: close performance gap on top-N hottest workloads","description":"## Why this exists

Placeholder epic for the optimization work that becomes actionable once franken_numpy-azql has measured the cross-engine performance gap. Today this epic has zero scope because we don't know where the gap is. Once the baseline exists, this epic will be split into one bead per hot workload that exceeds an agreed ratio threshold (set by the franken_numpy-ewze pivot decision).

## Background / context for future-self

- FrankenNumPy declares #![forbid(unsafe_code)] on ALL 9 crates. This is a hard constraint that rules out many classic optimization techniques: SIMD intrinsics via std::arch, unsafe transmute for zero-copy reinterpretation, raw pointer arithmetic for cache-line-aligned iteration, and FFI calls to BLAS/LAPACK. ALL optimizations under this epic MUST work within safe Rust.
- Safe-Rust-compatible optimization levers include: algorithm selection (better asymptotic complexity), data layout changes (SoA vs AoS), iterator combinators (chunks_exact for auto-vectorization hints), loop restructuring (tiling, blocking), allocation reduction (reuse buffers, arena patterns), branch elimination (branchless min/max), and const generics for monomorphization.
- The release profile already uses opt-level=3, LTO, single codegen unit, stripped symbols. These are global levers; per-workload optimization is about algorithmic and data-layout choices.
- Profiling tools needed: `cargo flamegraph` (requires perf on Linux), `samply` (sampling profiler), `criterion` (microbenchmark harness). Verify these are available on the build workers before starting child beads.
- The existing ~56% axis-reduction improvement (README L928) was achieved via a contiguous reduction kernel that avoids per-element index computation. This is the template for future optimizations: identify a structural inefficiency, implement one lever, prove correctness via the conformance suite.

## Optimization doctrine (from README L928 and AGENTS.md)

Every optimization bead under this epic MUST follow the documented "one-lever, proof-backed" pattern:

1. **Baseline** (p50/p95/p99 + memory) — already captured by franken_numpy-azql.
2. **Profile** the hotspot (cargo flamegraph / samply / criterion). Document which function/line is the bottleneck and what percentage of wall time it consumes.
3. **Implement ONE lever** (not three). State the lever clearly: "replaced Vec::extend with chunks_exact(8) for auto-vectorization" or "switched from NxN SVD to 2x2 fast path for detected small matrices."
4. **Prove behavior unchanged** via the full differential conformance suite (cargo test --workspace) + any workload-specific invariant checks.
5. **Re-baseline** and emit a delta artifact: {before: {p50, p95, p99}, after: {p50, p95, p99}, lever: "...", conformance_pass: true/false}. RaptorQ-protect the delta.
6. **Risk-note update** if the change touches a security-control boundary (e.g., parser optimization in fnp-io touches the malformed_npy_npz threat class).

## Safe-Rust optimization playbook (pre-identified levers)

These are CANDIDATE levers the child beads should consider. Not all will apply to every workload:

| Lever | Applicable to | Expected impact |
|-------|--------------|-----------------|
| `chunks_exact(N)` loops | Elementwise ops, reductions | Enables auto-vectorization without unsafe SIMD |
| Contiguous fast-path detection | All ops | Skip stride arithmetic when data is contiguous |
| Buffer reuse / arena allocation | Chained ops, einsum | Reduce allocator pressure |
| Branchless min/max | Reductions, sort comparisons | Eliminate branch misprediction |
| Tiled/blocked iteration | Matmul, 2D convolution | Improve cache locality |
| Specialized small-matrix paths | Linalg (3x3, 4x4) | Avoid general-NxN overhead for common sizes |
| Horner's method (already used) | Polynomials, Stirling | Minimal multiplications |
| Pre-sorted partition for percentile | Statistics | Avoid full sort when partial suffices |
| Lazy broadcast materialization | Broadcasted binary ops | Avoid allocating the broadcast output when it can be computed on-the-fly |

## Goals (post-baseline)

- After franken_numpy-azql AND franken_numpy-ewze close, file one child bead per workload whose ratio exceeds the agreed threshold T.
- Each child bead carries the 6-step doctrine above as its acceptance criteria.
- Each child bead specifies: which workload, current ratio, target ratio (at least halve the gap), candidate levers from the playbook, and profiling plan.
- Group related workloads into a single child bead when they share the same hot path (e.g., all contiguous elementwise ops likely share the same inner loop).
- Update CHANGELOG with delta-artifact links after each child bead closes.
- After all child beads close, re-run the full cross-engine benchmark (azql) and compare the new report to the pre-optimization baseline.

## Dependencies

- Blocked by franken_numpy-azql (need measurements) AND franken_numpy-ewze (need threshold decision and user approval to pivot).

## Acceptance criteria (for the epic itself)

- [ ] Child beads filed for every workload exceeding threshold T.
- [ ] Each child bead follows the 6-step doctrine.
- [ ] Each child bead has measurable before/after delta artifacts.
- [ ] Final re-run of azql shows improved ratios on targeted workloads.
- [ ] No regressions on non-targeted workloads (G7 gate passes throughout).
- [ ] Zero unsafe code introduced (forbid(unsafe_code) remains enforced).

## How this serves the over-arching goals

The README's "rigorous architecture" claim is partially backed today (correctness yes; performance unmeasured). Closing real perf gaps under the safe-Rust doctrine above is what turns the README from accurate-on-correctness into accurate-on-everything — while preserving the zero-unsafe-code guarantee that is a core differentiator.","status":"done","priority":2,"issue_type":"epic","estimated_minutes":120,"created_at":"2026-04-09T00:16:45.537679157Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-15T01:00:48.604731103Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["optimization","performance","phase3"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"franken_numpy-w4zs","depends_on_id":"franken_numpy-azql","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-09T00:16:45.537679157Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"franken_numpy-w4zs","depends_on_id":"franken_numpy-ewze","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-09T00:16:45.537679157Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"franken_numpy-w4zs.1","title":"Implement contiguous fast-path for elementwise binary ops","description":"Cross-engine benchmark shows 30-50x slowdown for elementwise binary ops on large contiguous arrays (add, sub, mul, div). The current implementation iterates element-by-element with float error checking on each iteration. 

Optimization opportunities:
1. Use chunks_exact(8) or similar for auto-vectorization
2. Skip float error checking when error mode is Ignore
3. Specialize Add/Mul/Div to avoid op.apply() match dispatch
4. Consider pre-checking for special values (NaN, Inf) to enable faster bulk path

Target: halve the gap (from 30-50x to 15-25x) as a first pass.

This is a child of franken_numpy-w4zs (optimization epic).","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","estimated_minutes":120,"created_at":"2026-04-15T00:15:47.343908659Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-15T00:21:23.782831465Z","closed_at":"2026-04-15T00:21:23.779393528Z","close_reason":"Implemented fast-path for elementwise binary ops: specialized loops for Add/Sub/Mul/Div, skip error checking when all modes are Ignore, use zip iteration with pre-allocated output for better cache locality","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["optimization","performance"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"franken_numpy-w4zs.1","depends_on_id":"franken_numpy-w4zs","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-04-15T00:15:47.343908659Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
